bids.reasoning: This is the opening bid at t=0, so I should start with my maximum utility offer. My highest utility comes from valueE across all issues. The most important issue is issueE (0.3 weight), followed by issueB and issueD (0.2 each), then issueC and issueA (0.1 each). I'll bid all valueE to maximize my utility at the start of negotiation.

The opponent opened with a very low-utility offer for me (issueA: valueA, issueB: valueA, issueC: valueB, issueD: valueA, issueE: valueB). My utility for that offer would be roughly: 0.1*0.20 + 0.2*0.20 + 0.1*0.73 + 0.2*0.42 + 0.3*0.31 ≈ 0.02 + 0.04 + 0.073 + 0.084 + 0.093 = 0.31, which is quite low. Their emotional message is cheap talk. We're very early (t=0.02), so I should stay close to my ideal bid. I'll repeat my top offer since the opponent has barely moved at all.

It's very early (t=0.04), so I can afford to stay firm near my top utility. The opponent is making highly self-serving offers (all valueA/valueB, which are terrible for me) while using emotional appeals - classic cheap talk. Their offers have barely moved (they switched issueE from valueB to valueA, which is actually worse for me). I should maintain my high-utility bid while perhaps making a very minor concession to signal willingness to negotiate.

The opponent has been making very low-utility offers for me (issueA: valueA, issueB: valueA, issueD: valueA which are all very low utility) and hasn't budged at all - their offers are essentially their best possible outcomes. Their emotional messages are cheap talk contradicted by the numbers. We're only at t=0.06, so there's no pressure to concede yet. I'll stay near my optimal bid but make a tiny concession on low-weight issues (issueA, issueC) to show some movement.
